en.extract:
The  Papuan spinetail (Mearnsia novaeguineae), also known as the Papuan needletail, New Guinea spine-tailed swift or Papuan spine-tailed swift, is a species of swift native to the island of New Guinea.


Description
M. novaeguineae is a small (11.5 cm in length), stocky swift with a short, rounded tail. Tail spines extend up to 1 mm beyond the web of the tail.  Head and upperparts glossy green-black, grey-throat with a white underside. Wings display hooked ends, and are widest at the middle before tapering into the body.


Distribution
Endemic to New Guinea where it is widespread in the lowlands and hills up to 550 m.  In 2004, the species was first recorded in Australia when three individuals were observed on Boigu Island, Queensland.


Habitat
Forest edges, gardens and cleared areas with standing dead trees.


Food
Flying insects.


Breeding
Nests in high tree hollows, and may partially construct nest out of dry palm fibres.


Conservation
Common and widespread species assessed as being of Least Concern, though population size appears to be decreasing.


References

Beehler, Bruce M.; & Finch, Brian W. (1985). Species Checklist of the Birds of New Guinea. RAOU Monograph No.1. Royal Australasian Ornithologists Union: Melbourne. ISBN 0-9599823-2-9
Beehler, Bruce M.; Pratt, Thane K.; & Zimmerman, Dale A. (1986). Birds of New Guinea. Wau Ecology Handbook No.9. Princeton University Press. ISBN 0-691-02394-8
Coates, Brian J. (1985). The Birds of Papua New Guinea. Volume 1: Non-Passerines. Dover Publications: Alderley, Queensland. ISBN 0-9590257-0-7

The  '''Papuan spinetail''' ('''''Mearnsia novaeguineae'''''), also known as the '''Papuan needletail''', '''New Guinea spine-tailed swift''' or '''Papuan spine-tailed swift''', is a species of [[Swift (bird)|swift]] native to the island of [[New Guinea]].

== Description ==
''M. novaeguineae'' is a small (11.5 cm in length), stocky [[Swift (bird)|swift]] with a short, rounded tail. Tail spines extend up to 1 mm beyond the web of the tail.  Head and upperparts glossy green-black, grey-throat with a white underside. Wings display hooked ends, and are widest at the middle before tapering into the body.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=Chantler |first=P. |last2=Boesman |first2=P.F.D. |date=2020 |editor-last=del Hoyo |editor-first=A. Elliott |editor2-last=Sargatal |editor2-first=J. |editor3-last=Christie |editor3-first=D.A. |editor4-last=de Juana |editor4-first=E. |title=Papuan Spinetail (''Mearnsia novaeguineae'') |url=https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.papnee1.01 |website=Birds of the World |publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology |place=Ithaca, NY}}</ref>

==Distribution==
Endemic to [[New Guinea]] where it is widespread in the lowlands and hills up to 550 m.<ref>{{Cite web |last=BirdLife International |date=2024 |title=Species factsheet: Papuan Spinetail ''Mearnsia novaeguineae'' |url=https://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/papuan-spinetail-mearnsia-novaeguineae |website=DataZone by BirdLife}}</ref>  In 2004, the species was first recorded in Australia when three individuals were observed on [[Boigu Island]], Queensland.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Clarke |first=Rohan H. |date=2006 |title=Papuan Spine-tailed Swifts ''Mearnsia novaeguineae'' on Boigu Island, Torres Strait, Queensland |url=https://research.monash.edu/en/publications/papuan-spine-tailed-swifts-imearnsia-novaeguineaei-on-boigu-islan/ |journal=Australian Field Ornithology |volume=23 |issue=3 |pages=125-129}}</ref>

==Habitat==
Forest edges, gardens and cleared areas with standing dead trees.<ref name=":0" />

==Food==
Flying insects.

==Breeding==
Nests in high [[tree hollow]]s, and may partially construct nest out of dry palm fibres.<ref name=":0" />

==Conservation==
Common and widespread species assessed as being of [[Least Concern]], though population size appears to be decreasing.<ref name="iucn status"/>

==References==
{{Reflist}}
* Beehler, Bruce M.; & Finch, Brian W. (1985). ''Species Checklist of the Birds of New Guinea''. RAOU Monograph No.1. Royal Australasian Ornithologists Union: Melbourne. {{ISBN|0-9599823-2-9}}
* Beehler, Bruce M.; Pratt, Thane K.; & Zimmerman, Dale A. (1986). ''Birds of New Guinea''. Wau Ecology Handbook No.9. Princeton University Press. {{ISBN|0-691-02394-8}}
* Coates, Brian J. (1985). ''The Birds of Papua New Guinea. Volume 1: Non-Passerines''. Dover Publications: Alderley, Queensland. {{ISBN|0-9590257-0-7}}
